bibliographic_data.title: Using Data Mining for Citation Analysis
bibliographic_data.abstract: This paper presents a new model for citation analysis, applying new methodological approaches in citation studies. These methods are demonstrated by an analysis of cited references from publications by the Geological Sciences faculty at the University of Colorado Boulder. The author made use of simple Python scripting, the Web of Science API, and OpenRefine to examine the most frequently cited journals and compare them to library holdings data to discover materials absent from the local collection. Of the more than 20,000 citations analyzed, 80 percent cited approximately 10 percent of all titles (412 journals). A notable finding was the heavy reliance of faculty members upon works between zero and two years of age. The streamlined model presented here removes the constraints of time and effort encountered by academic librarians interested in conducting citation analyses.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper introduces a new method for citation analysis using data mining techniques, specifically Python scripting and Web of Science API, to streamline the process and reduce time and effort required by librarians.
core_content.key_methodology: The author used simple Python scripting, the Web of Science API, and OpenRefine to analyze more than 20,000 citations from publications by Geological Sciences faculty at the University of Colorado Boulder. The analysis aimed to identify frequently cited journals and gaps in the library collection.
outcomes: Identified that 80 percent of the citations analyzed came from approximately 10 percent (412 journals) of all titles.
Found a heavy reliance on works between zero and two years old by faculty members.
Developed a streamlined, programmatic methodology for collecting citation data.
core_content.full_text_markdown: This paper introduces a new approach to citation analysis using data mining techniques. The author applied these methods to analyze over 20,000 citations from publications by the Geological Sciences faculty at the University of Colorado Boulder. By leveraging Python scripting and the Web of Science API, the study aimed to identify frequently cited journals and gaps in the library collection. Key findings included that 80 percent of the citations came from about 10 percent (412 journals) of all titles, with a notable reliance on works between zero and two years old by faculty members. The research also developed a streamlined methodology for collecting citation data.
